journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science

title-group.article-title: A retrospective study on subcutaneous malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors in 14 domestic rabbits (Oryctolagus cuniculus) treated surgically between 2008 and 2021

contrib-group.aff: 2)Veterinary Pathology, Graduate School of Agricultural and Life Science, The University of Tokyo, Tokyo, Japan
3)VISION VETS GROUP Lab, Animal Medical Center Peco, Tokyo, Japan

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: Nakata M: azu05pooh@hotmail.com, Miwa Exotic Animal Hospital, 1-25-5 Komagome, Toshima-ku, Tokyo 170-0003, Japan

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2024 The Japanese Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: A retrospective study involving 14 pet rabbits histopathologically diagnosed with malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors (MPNSTs) was conducted. The age at diagnosis was 4–12 years, with a median age of 8.6 years. All rabbits had solid subcutaneous tumor masses in varied locations. Surgical excision of the tumors was performed in all cases. Recurrence was observed in 10 cases (71%), and postoperative metastasis to the lung was suspected in 4 cases (29%). The postoperative mean and median survival times were 11 months and 9 months, respectively. Hence, MPNSTs should be considered in the differential diagnosis for subcutaneous masses in rabbits and it is essential to inform the owners of the potentially high recurrence and metastasis rates.

body.p: Peripheral nerve sheath tumors (PNSTs) are a heterogeneous group of soft tissue tumors originating from peripheral nerve cells, such as Schwann cells, fibroblasts, or perineural cells [4, 5, 13]. PNSTs are classified as benign or malignant based on their morphological features and biological behavior veterinary fields [2,3,4, 14]. Histopathologically, malignant PNSTs (MPNSTs) comprise wavy spindle cells arranged in bundles and whorls, similar to those in benign PNSTs; however, they also exhibit cellular atypia and an aggressive local invasive tendency [4]. Notably, MPNSTs were diagnosed in 8 out of 179 rabbits in a retrospective study of cutaneous neoplasms in pet rabbits [17], whereas benign PNSTs have been reported in two separate cases [10, 12].
Due to the lack of detailed information on this rare tumor type, this study aimed to describe the clinical characteristics, treatment outcomes, and prognosis of MPNSTs in pet rabbits.

We retrospectively reviewed the medical records of rabbits subjected to surgery at a veterinary hospital and histopathologically diagnosed with MPNST between January 2008 and December 2021. Fourteen rabbits were identified and met the inclusion criteria of the study. A summary of the 14 cases is presented in Table 1Table 1.Summary of 14 cases of malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors in rabbits
Patient #SignalmentTumor locationSurgery performedRecurrenceTime before recurrence (months)Suspected metastasisSurvival time (months)
17y F (spayed)Scapular①②③Tumour excisionYes420
29y MScapularAmputation8
310y MAxilla①②Tumour excisionYes2Unknown (≥7)
410y MScapularTumour excisionYes3YesUnknown (≥3)
58.3y M (castrated)AbdominalTumour excision28
66.7y MForelimbAmputationYes3
74.3y MThoracic①②Tumour excisionYes34
810.3y MAbdominalTumour excisionYesUnknown (≥1)
99.8y MThoracic①Tumour excisionYes2Yes4
②Tumour debulking
106.5y F (spayed)AxillaAmputationYes2Unknown (≥2)
116.3y MForelimb①Tumour excisionYes <110
②Amputation
1212y F (spayed)Forelimb①AmputationYes1216
②Tumour excision
139.7y MInguinalTumour excisionYes <1Unknown (≥2)
147.5y MScapular①②Tumour excisionYes39
③Amputation
Numbers in front of the surgery description indicate the chronology if multiple surgeries were performed. Generally, a surgical margin of approximately 2–3 cm was aimed for when feasible. ‘Time before recurrence’ indicates the period before tumor recurrence, and it was recorded after the first surgery. Both “Time before recurrence” and “Survival time” were indicated in months.. The age range at the time of diagnosis was 4–12 years, with mean and median ages of 8.4 years and 8.6 years, respectively. The proportion of affected male rabbits was more than that of female rabbits (11 vs. 3). All the 3 females were spayed, whereas only 1 of the males was castrated.

All the patients presented with solid subcutaneous masses adhering to the underlying soft tissue. The lesions appeared ulcerated in 4 patients (29%), of which 2 reported discomfort with disease progression. The tumors were located in the scapular region (n=4, 29%), limbs (n=3, 21%), abdominal and inguinal region (n=3, 21%), thoracic region (n=2, 14%), and axilla (n=2, 14%) (Fig. 1Fig. 1.
Presentation of malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors as subcutaneous masses in various locations: (A) Scapular region; (B) Limb; (C) Abdominal and inguinal region; (D) Thoracic region; (E) Axilla. Note: The masses are indicated by asterisks (*).). Preoperative radiographs showed no signs of lung metastasis in all cases.

Surgical resection of the tumor was attempted in all cases with either tumor excision and/or amputation of the affected limb based on the tumor location. Recurrence was noted in 10 of 14 rabbits after the first surgery, of which 7 rabbits underwent a second surgery of either tumor excision (6/7 cases; #1, #3, #7, #9, #12, and #14) or amputation (1/7 cases; #11). Recurrence after the second surgery was noted in 4 of the 7 rabbits (#1, #3, #12, and #14), and the mean and median times before recurrence after the first surgery were 3 months and 2 months, respectively. A third surgery, either tumor excision (#1) or amputation (#14), was performed for 2 rabbits. Among those who underwent amputation during the first surgery, recurrence at the amputation site was noted in 2 out of 4 rabbits.

Two rabbits received radiation therapy with an orthovoltage irradiation unit (6 Gy, once a week) approximately 1 month after the second surgery. One rabbit received radiation as a preventative measure for recurrence after complete tumor excision; however, it died of unknown causes the day after the second irradiation was performed. The other patient received radiation as adjunctive therapy because of the enlarging tumor size noted shortly after partial resection of the recurrent lesion. Despite a decrease in tumor size, the patient died of renal failure caused by inappetence 6 days after the second irradiation was performed.

Tumor metastasis to the lungs was suspected in 4 rabbits (29%) based on the clinical signs and thoracic radiography or computed tomography findings within 1–3 months after the first surgery. Nodular radiopacities considered to be pulmonary metastasis were seen in these patients. Excluding the 5 cases that were lost to follow-up, the mean and median survival times of the remaining 9 cases were 11 months and 9 months, respectively. Most patients (6/9, 67%) died within a year of the first surgery, and the cause of death remained unclear.

Histopathologically, tumors in all cases were diagnosed as malignant based on their infiltrative growth. The tumor developed continuously from the peripheral nerve tissue and infiltrated the subcutaneous tissue and skeletal muscles (Fig. 2A and 2BFig. 2.
Histological images of malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors in rabbits. A (11) and B (6): Tumor was developed continuously from peripheral nerve tissue (arrows). Insets show high magnification of spindle neoplastic cells. Bars=500 μm. C (13): Tumor tissue has a mucinous stroma and infiltrates the surrounding tissue. Insets show high magnification of spindle neoplastic cells. Bar=200 μm. D (14): Bundles of neoplastic spindle cells show nuclear palisading. Bar=50 μm. Note: The number following each alphabet indicates patient number of which the image was obtained from.). The tumor tissue was composed of neoplastic spindle cells and mucinous or collagenous stroma (Fig. 2C). Neoplastic cells were arranged in bundles, streams, and whorls, with occasional nuclear palisading (Fig. 2D). Nuclear atypia and anisokaryosis were moderate, and vascular invasion was not observed in any of the examined cases. Since the animals were rabbits and antibodies that are raised in rabbits are not suitable for immunohistochemical examinations, we were unable to perform immunohistochemistry to support morphological diagnosis, which is a limitation of this study.

Malignant PNSTs are rarely reported in domestic animals other than dogs [15]. In dogs, middle-aged to older patients are usually affected, with a mean age of 9 years [4]. In this study, the mean age of the rabbits at diagnosis was 8.4 years, showing a similar tendency. According to the sex distribution in this study, the incidence was higher in male rabbits (79%) than in female rabbits, as observed in most of the previous studies on dogs [4, 7].

In dogs, approximately 55% of MPNSTs develop in the nerve plexus and distal peripheral nerves, whereas 45% develop in the spinal roots and cranial nerves [6, 9,10,11]. The most common initial clinical signs in dogs include mild and chronic progressive lameness [1], depending on the initial location and function of the surrounding tissues involved. With disease progression, muscular atrophy, hyporeflexia, palpable masses, and pain on palpation have also been observed [4]. In contrast, the rabbits in this study showed almost no clinical signs other than a solid subcutaneous mass. The lesions were distributed throughout the body. In a retrospective study of cutaneous neoplasms in rabbits, the MPNSTs identified in eight rabbits were located in the pinna, head, neck, axilla, abdominal wall, and limbs [17]. Therefore, it is implied that MPNSTs in rabbits may have a tendency to affect the nerves distal to the neural plexus.

The treatment options for MPNSTs in dogs include surgical intervention and radiation therapy [10]. Although complete surgical excision with wide margins has been proven curative in most subcutaneous neoplasms in dogs, the size and location of the tumor and the small stature of the rabbits may hinder complete excision [4, 10]. Additionally, the biological behavior of MPNSTs makes recurrence common after excision [4]. Recurrence was identified in 10 out of 14 rabbits (71%) in this study, regardless of wide-margin tumor resection, leading to repeated tumor excision or ultimately limb amputation. Radiation therapy has achieved satisfactory results in several cases of neoplasms in rabbits [10, 16]. In this study, the potential effectiveness of radiation therapy against the tumor was highlighted in 1 of the 2 rabbits subjected to radiation therapy. However, since only 2 rabbits were irradiated in this study, the effect of radiation therapy on MPNSTs in rabbits is currently unclear.

Prognosis, including the survival time and recurrence rate, of dogs with MPNSTs depends on the histological grade, mitotic rate, and tumor location [4, 8]. The mean survival times in dogs with MPNSTs of the nerve plexus and nerve root have been reported as 12 months and 5 months, respectively. Thus, the closer the tumor is to the spinal cord, the poorer is the prognosis [1, 8]. Prognostic factors for MPNSTs in rabbits have not yet been established. The mean survival time in this study was 11 months, which was similar to that observed in dogs with MPNSTs of the nerve plexus. Despite their locally invasive behavior, systemic metastasis of MPNSTs is rarely observed in dogs [4]. However, in this study, the metastatic rate was relatively high, with pulmonary metastasis suspected in 4 out of 14 cases (29%).

Based on the findings of this study, malignant PNSTs should be considered in the differential diagnosis of subcutaneous masses in rabbits, along with other sarcomas such as fibrosarcoma, myxosarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, and so on. It is essential to perform radical resection with a wide margin and inform the owners of the potentially high recurrence and metastasis rates in rabbits diagnosed with MPNSTs. Finally, collecting sufficient clinical data in the future is warranted to understand the long-term prognosis of malignant PNSTs better in rabbits.

sec.title: CONFLICTS OF INTEREST
sec.p: The authors declare there are no conflicts of interest.
